channel.title: Yukon on Здесь будет название
channel.description: Recent content in Yukon on Здесь будет название

item.title: The Wit of Porportuk
item.description:
El-Soo was the most beautiful woman in the Yukon, and Porportuk was the richest man. The collision of these two facts would create a story that the old-timers told for years afterward, shaking their heads at the strange ways of love and money.

Porportuk had made his fortune in the traditional way — trading with the white men, accumulating wealth while his people grew poorer. He was old and fat and ugly, but he had power, and power in the North meant everything.

item.title: The League of Old Men
item.description:
The league of old men met in secret, their council fire hidden in a valley that the white men had not yet discovered. They were the elders of the Whitefish tribe, and they had gathered to discuss a matter of grave importance — the survival of their people.

Imber was the youngest of the old men, though his hair was white and his joints ached with the cold. He had lived through the coming of the white men, had watched the slow destruction of everything his people held sacred. Now he stood before his brothers to propose a desperate plan.

item.title: The Master of Mystery
item.description:
The master of mystery — that’s what the prospectors called him, the old Tlingit shaman who lived alone in the hills above Dawson. He spoke with spirits, they said. He could cure diseases that baffled white doctors. He knew the future, or at least he claimed to.

His real name was unpronounceable by English speakers, a complex series of clicks and tones that meant something about ravens and mountains. The miners simply called him Old Tom, which he accepted with tolerant amusement.

item.title: The Men of Forty Mile
item.description:
The men of Forty Mile were a rough lot, even by the standards of the goldfields. This small settlement on the Yukon River was one of the earliest mining camps in the territory, established before the great rush of ‘98 brought thousands of newcomers flooding into the region.

These old-timers — the sourdoughs, as they called themselves — had little patience for the cheechakos (newcomers) who arrived with fancy equipment and fancier ideas. They had learned the ways of the North through hard experience, and they viewed the greenhorns with a mixture of contempt and pity.

item.title: The Ghost Dog of the Klondike
item.description:
They called him the Ghost Dog now — the Yeehats, the Indians, the few white men who had glimpsed him in the wilderness. He was a legend, a creature of myth and shadow, spoken of in whispers around campfires throughout the Klondike.

Buck had become what the call had always promised. He ran with the wolves now, a full member of the pack. But he was more than just another wolf. He was larger, stronger, more intelligent. When the pack hunted, he led. When they rested, they gathered around him. When they howled at the moon, his voice rose above all others.
